S2 File¶

SOS-ALL Machine Learning Methods¶

In [5]:
import os
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
from IPython.display import display, Image

1. Data¶

  • Base-file for the analysis is DataTransformed_ImmunoCAP_Final_value_report_f_dtypes.csv received from Damir Zhakparov / Katja Baerenfaller. The file was used in combination with DataTransformed_ImmunoCAP_Final.txt

data = pd.read_csv(data_path, sep='\t', index_col=0)
data_setup = pd.read_csv(path_data_setup, sep=";", index_col=0)

139 rows × 7 columns

1.1 Removing unnecessary features¶

All features with a value=1 in the 'exclude'column of the file DataTransformed_ImmunoCAP_Final.txt were excluded from all subsequent analyses. Some features were excluded after initial analyses and subsequent discussions as part of an iterative process. In total, the following 45 features were excluded:

['vaccination_status', 'antibiotic_exposure_age', 'solidfood_which_first', 'peanuts_exposure_age_first', 'peanuts_exposure_regularity', 'othernuts_exposure_age_first', 'othernuts_exposure_regularity', 'fish_exposure_age_first', 'fish_exposure_regularity', 'eczema_ever', 'eczema_age', 'eczema_whodiagnosed', 'medication_any', 'medication_nasalcorticosteroid', 'medication_antihistamines', 'medication_steroidcreams', 'medication_oral_which', 'medication_antihistamines_last', 'medicalhistory_comorbidities', 'parental_education_level', 'parental_income', 'am_height', 'am_weight', 'am_abd_girth', 'am_skinfold', 'scorad', 'bsa', 'country', 'familyhistory_sibling_eczema', 'familyhistory_sibling_other_allergic_disease', 'diagnosis_location', 'RNASeq', 'location', 'log_blood_counts_wcc', 'stool_katokatz', 'sp_egg', 'sp_milk', 'sp_soy', 'sp_wheat', 'sp_fish', 'sp_peanut', 'sp_hazelnut', 'sp_fresh_peanut', 'sp_fresh_egg', 'sp_fresh_milk']

1.2 Setting feature types ¶

Features were converted to the appropriate data types based on the "dtype" column in DataTransformed_ImmunoCAP_Final.txt column 'dtype'. The "PID" column was set as the index for the analysis and was not used as a predictive feature.

1.3 Shuffling observations¶

All observations were shuffled to ensure that training and validation sets do not contain only one group of data.

1.4 Setting the target variable¶

The "diagnosis" column was defined as the target variable (y). The values were recoded from "AD" and "HC" to 1 (AD) and 0 (HC), respectively.

1.5 Final training data¶

After applying all the steps described above, the training data were structured as follows:

No train–test split was performed, as all data were used for model development via cross-validation. The dataset was therefore used for both training and validation of all models.

2. Modelling¶

The goal of the analysis was not simply to build the "best" model (and obtain a score), but to identify useful and meaningful features that are important for model building. Ideally, these features should be relatively stable across different models and/or model groups. This would indicate that they are true predictors rather than being specific to a particular model (or its hyperparameters). To achieve this, several steps were implemented.

2.1 Optuna (framework)¶

Optuna was used as a framework to perform hyperparameter optimisation (https://optuna.org/). This framework allows the definition of search spaces for hyperparameters, rather than fixed values as in grid search. After defining these hyperparameter spaces, either a number of trials or a time budget is specified. Based on these settings, Optuna attempts to identify the "best" model using a TPESampler. Unlike grid search, where all possible combinations of hyperparameter settings are evaluated, the TPE approach focuses on parameter settings that are more likely to achieve high performance. TPE stands for Tree-structured Parzen Estimator. On each trial, for each parameter, TPE fits one Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) l(x) to the set of parameter values associated with the best objective values, and another GMM g(x) to the remaining parameter values. It then selects the parameter value x that maximises the ratio l(x)/g(x) (source: https://optuna.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/samplers/generated/optuna.samplers.TPESampler.html).

The TPESampler was applied not only to optimise hyperparameters within a single model, but also across different models (algorithms). In this study, the following algorithms (and corresponding feature spaces) were used:

  • RandomForestClassifier (sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier):

    params = {
            'n_estimators': trial.suggest_int('rf__n_estimators', 50, 500),
            'max_depth': trial.suggest_categorical('rf__max_depth', [None, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
        }

  • MLP (sklearn.neural_network.MLPClassifier):

    params = {
            'hidden_layer_sizes': tuple([trial.suggest_int(f'mlp__layer_{i}_size', 1, 200) for i in
                                         range(trial.suggest_int('mlp__n_layers', 1, 5))]),
            'alpha': trial.suggest_float('mlp__alpha', 0.00001, 0.001, log=False),
            'activation': trial.suggest_categorical('mlp__activation', ['logistic', 'tanh', 'relu']),
            'solver': trial.suggest_categorical('mlp__solver', ['lbfgs', 'sgd', 'adam'])
        }

  • GNB (sklearn.naive_bayes.GaussianNB)

    params = {
            'var_smoothing': trial.suggest_float('gnb__var_smoothing', 1e-10, 1e-7, log=True) 
        }

  • XGBOOST (xgboost.XGBClassifier)

    params = {
            'max_depth': trial.suggest_int('xgb__max_depth', 1, 9),
            'learning_rate': trial.suggest_float('xgb__learning_rate', 0.01, 1.0, log=True),
            'n_estimators': trial.suggest_int('xgb__n_estimators', 50, 500),
            'min_child_weight': trial.suggest_int('xgb__min_child_weight', 1, 10),
            'gamma': trial.suggest_float('xgb__gamma', 1e-8, 1.0, log=True),
            'subsample': trial.suggest_float('xgb__subsample', 0.01, 1.0, log=True),
            'colsample_bytree': trial.suggest_float('xgb__colsample_bytree', 0.01, 1.0, log=True),
            'reg_alpha': trial.suggest_float('xgb__reg_alpha', 1e-8, 1.0, log=True),
            'reg_lambda': trial.suggest_float('xgb__reg_lambda', 1e-8, 1.0, log=True)
        }

2.2 Pipeline¶

The training data currently contain missing values. For some models, these missing values need to be replaced with valid numerical values to allow model training. In addition, preprocessing steps such as normalisation or scaling are useful for certain algorithms, for example MLP. To account for these requirements and to reduce the risk of data leakage, an sklearn.pipeline was constructed.

2.2.1 Imputation¶

Imputation of missing values is one of the most important steps in the prediction process in this study. Replacing missing values with simple group means or medians, as commonly done, would lead to distorted results in this case. This is because missing values are not equally distributed between "AD" and "HC", and for several features there is a clear difference in the number of missing values between these groups. If missing values are replaced by a constant value (e.g. group mean or median), the model may use this as an additional signal, leading to biased predictions. For this reason, the miceforest package was used. This imputation method applies a RandomForest-based approach to estimate missing values for each feature based on the values of other features.

The following parameters were used for miceforest:

   mice_initialization = trial.suggest_categorical('mice_init', ['random', 'empty'])
   mice_iterations = trial.suggest_int("mice_iter", 1, 5)

The initialisation can be either empty or random, and between 1 and 5 iterations are applied to estimate missing values for each feature.

2.2.2 Scaling¶

Some algorithms perform better when features are scaled. Therefore, the pipeline included a scaling step with the following parameters:

    trial.suggest_categorical('scaler', ['StandardScaler', 'MinMaxScaler', 'passthrough'])

passthrough indicates that no scaling was applied

2.2.3 Quasi-constant features¶

Quasi-constant features are features with no or very little variation and therefore provide little useful information. These features were excluded from the analysis. They could not be removed during preprocessing, as the lack of variation depends on the train–validation split during cross-validation.

    trial.suggest_categorical('quasi_constant', ['variance_threshold', 'passthrough'])

In case the 'variance_threshold' is used be the Sampler (see next section) the parameter-space for 'variance_threshold' is:

    params = {
                'threshold': trial.suggest_float('quasi_constant__variance_threshold', 0, 0.15),
            }

2.2.5 Feature selection¶

The training data contain a large number of features, which increases the risk of overfitting when all features are used. Therefore, a feature selection step was included in the pipeline.

    sklearn.feature_selection.SelectKBestSelectKBest(k), with k=trial.suggest_int("selection_kbest_features", 5, 30)

This means that at least 5 features and at most 30 features were selected in each model run.

After applying all steps, the pipeline was structured as follows:

 pipe = Pipeline([
                ('mice_imputer', 'mice_imputer'),
                ('knn_imputer', knn_imputer),
                ('scaler', scaler),
                ('selection', selection),
                ('quasi_constant', constant),
                ('model', model)
            ])

Remark: The knn_imputer applied after the mice_imputer is necessary because, in some cases (depending on the train-test split and very few missing values in a feature), it is possible that not missing values are present in the training data. In such cases, the mice_imputer in cannot estimate missing values for that feature. To address this, an additional nn_imputer imputation step was applied to ensure that all missing values are replaced. This issue affects only very few values (if any), therefore the knn_imputer can be used without introducing the data leakage mentioned above.

2.3 Cross-validation¶

The entire pipeline was evaluated for each hyperparameter combination using a 5-fold cross-validation process. The mean accuracy across the five test folds was used as the final score for each combination. Accuracy was chosen as the evaluation metric because the distribution of "AD" and "HC" in the dataset is nearly balanced.

3. Results¶

In this study, 77 different models (with different hyperparameter settings) were evaluated. All models were stored so they could be analysed later using SHAP values.

Remark: Each model was refitted on the full dataset using its respective hyperparameter settings after cross-validation. The cross-validation score therefore serves as an indicator of how well the model is expected to perform on unseen data (accuracy). SHAP values were calculated using the full dataset.

The best accuracy score (0.81) was achieved by multiple Random Forest models.

In [12]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/overview.png", width='75%'))

The best model performance was achieved after 52 trials.

In [13]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/model_performance_development.png", width='75%'))

Interestingly, most (almost all) well-performing Random Forest (RF) models have a max_depth parameter of only 1, resulting in very simple trees with only one split.

In [14]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/rf_52_max_depth.png", width='75%'))

As also visible in the overview plot, the model was the most important parameter in the hyperparameter feature space. Many different Random Forest models achieved high scores. Random Forest outperformed the other model groups in this run.

In [15]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/hyperparamter_importance.png", width='75%'))

To compare feature stability, the 3 best models from each model group were selected. For all these combinations (4 groups × 3 models), feature stability (method = Jaccard) was calculated and plotted.

Except for trial 48 (XGBOOST) and GBN (trial 28), high scores (> 0.6) were achieved. This is a good indicator that the features do not differ much between the individual trials.

In [16]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_stability_jaccard.png", width='75%'))

The table above shows that a couple of features (9) were present in all (3-best) models.

These have been:

fuel_heating_Wood_Coal
total_IgE
fuel_cooking_Electricity_Gas
fuel_cooking_Paraffin_Stove
fuel_cooking_Open_fires_outside
sIgE_Der
sIgE_fish
sIgG4_Der
henegg_exposure_regularity
hayfever_ever

Also, other features have a great overlap across all models, as can be seen in the table above.

This image shows the Jaccard score for feature stability per model group. The 3 dots per group represent the comparison of all 3 models in this particular group (e.g. RF1 vs RF2, RF1 vs RF3, and RF2 vs RF3). RandomForest (RF) reached the highest scores in feature stability. A Jaccard score of 1 means that all features are present in the compared models.

In [18]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/jaccard_overview.png", width='75%'))

As next step in the analysis, the SHAP values (https://shap.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) for the most promising models were calculated. SHAP values are very handy for explaining models, even if they are "black boxes" (for example MLP). The concept originally comes from game theory, to calculate the contribution of each player (of a game) to the final result. This idea can also be transferred to the importance of features in a classification model.

3.1 SHAP values¶

3.1.1 Model: ID52 (RandomForest)¶

The feature importance calculated with SHAP values in Model52 (best model overall) looks like this:

In [19]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_52_shap.png", width='75%'))

And feature importance using feature permutation:

In [20]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_52_permutation.png", width='75%'))

The first 3 features are the same in both analyses, with a different ranking, as can be seen in the plots above:

  • sIgE_Der
  • fuel_cooking_Electricity_Gas
  • sIgE_egg
In [21]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/shap_detailed_summary_52.png", width='60%'))

In the SHAP detailed view, the effects on each feature can be analysed:

Very obvious is the effect of a low/high value in fuel_cooking_Electricity_Gas. Observations with value = 1 (high) increase their SHAP value, and value = 0 (low) decreases the SHAP value. So observations with value = 1 in fuel_cooking_Electricity_Gas tend to have an increased possibility of having diagnosis 1 (= AD). The opposity seems to be the case for henegg_exposure_regularity and fuel_cooking_paraffin_stove. In total_IgE, higher feature values seem to correspond to higher SHAP values and therefore a bigger chance of having a diagnosisof AD.

3.1.2 Model: ID34 (XGBOOST)¶

The feature importance calculated with SHAP values in Model34 (best model overall) looks like this:

In [22]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_34_shap.png", width='75%'))

And feature importance using feature permutation:

In [23]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_34_permutation.png", width='75%'))

SHAP detailed view (first 8 features):

In [24]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/shap_detailed_summary_34.png", width='60%'))

3.1.3 Model: ID14 (MLP)¶

The feature importance calculated with SHAP values in Model14 (best model overall) looks like this:

In [25]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_14_shap.png", width='75%'))

And feature importance using feature permutation:

In [26]:
display(Image(filename="data/output/feature_importance_14_permutation.png", width='75%'))

Interestingly feature sIgE_Der that seems to be very important for RF and XGBOOST isn't that important for a neuronal network. The feature distributions seems to be a little different in case of MLP.

SHAP detailed view (first 8 features):
